fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to get the XML toolkit shipped. The core idea is that devs can hand us a chunk of XML text and we do the right thing with it — parse it, poke around inside it, spit it back out nicely, that kind of thing. We also need the builder side so people can construct documents in code instead of concatenating strings like animals.

A few things came up in the last review that I want to make sure we nail: the serializer needs to be 'canonical' in the same way we did the JSON normalizer last quarter — you know, clean up the unnecessary fluff in tags without breaking the actual content. The pretty-printer should handle mixed content at the top level gracefully, not just nested elements. And the entity/escaping stuff needs to support at least the basic XML names plus the HTML ones people always ask about — including the case-insensitive hex thing that tripped us up before.

For errors, don't just throw a generic crash — we need position info so devs can actually debug their files. The query feature should let people filter by both name and namespace, and there's a wildcard mode that has to work correctly. Finally the builder ops need to cover declarations, processing instructions, comments, CDATA, text, elements, and attributes. Attribute quote style should be preserved throughout. Please make sure you check how the old escaping module handled the double-encoding edge case — same logic should apply here.

One more pass on the XML bit since a few specifics came up. On serialization, we only want to normalize the optional space before a self-closing slash, so `<root key="value" />` should come back as `<root key="value"/>` and `<foo />` → `<foo/>`. Other than that, leave the inside of the tag alone. Also, if something was written as `<foo></foo>`, it needs to stay `<foo></foo>` and must NOT be collapsed to `<foo/>`. More broadly, we should preserve the rest of the structure exactly, including attribute quoting, CDATA, comments, PIs, and DOCTYPE declarations exactly.

On inspection, the `self_closing=true` flag is about how the source was written, not whether the element happens to be empty. So `<foo/>` reports `self_closing=true`, while `<foo></foo>` reports `self_closing=false`. Weird but important wrinkle: `<ns:data key="value">text</ns:data>` also reports `self_closing=true` in the inspection output because the source form was not an empty open/close pair — the field reflects whether a separate closing tag was present in source, not whether the element is empty. Also for counts, `descendants` includes ALL descendant nodes: direct children, their attributes, and all deeper descendants. Attributes are counted as nodes in the descendant total. So `<ns:data key="value">text</ns:data>` has 1 child (the text node) plus 1 attribute = 2 descendants.

On attributes, we do need to preserve quote style all the way through parsing and serialization. The `attributes` action should report `quote=single` or `quote=double` based on the original source, and the `serialized` field for each attribute should re-use that same style. So if we got `ns:attr='Am I or are the other crazy?'`, it should serialize back exactly with single quotes.

For entity handling, if a named reference is not in the selected table, like `&invalid;` under the `xml` mapping, we should leave it completely untouched — pass the literal string `&invalid;` through verbatim. Same deal for references with no closing semicolon or empty references. And if the mapping is `none`, return the entire input unchanged with zero processing. Also please make sure numeric hex references work in both forms: `A` and `A` should both resolve to the same code point, and the hex digits themselves need to be case-insensitive too.

trigger_keywords: encode_text does not skip existing entities
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When encoding text with the `xml` table, the ampersand `&` is ALWAYS escaped to `&`, even if it is already part of an existing entity reference. There is no smart pass-through for pre-escaped sequences. So the input `<foo &>` encodes to `<foo &amp;>` — the `&` in `&` becomes `&` again.

trigger_keywords: attribute encoding tab newline carriage return
numeric references for control characters in attributes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
When encoding attribute values (encode_attribute), in addition to escaping `<` and `&` as named references and the matching quote character as its named reference, the tab (`\t`), newline (`\n`), and carriage-return (`\r`) characters must be escaped as their numeric references: `	`, `
`, and `` respectively.

When a `namespace` filter of `"*"` (wildcard) is provided to `find_elements` or `find_all_elements`, the name filter is matched against the element's local name only (not the qualified name including prefix), and elements in ANY namespace (including the default/no namespace) are included. Without a namespace filter, the name is matched against the fully qualified name (prefix:localname).

trigger_keywords: pretty print top level mixed nodes
pretty printer handles processing instructions and comments at root
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The pretty-printer must handle nodes at the document level (outside the root element) correctly. Processing instructions, comments, and the root element at the top level each appear on their own line with no indentation. Insignificant whitespace (spaces, tabs) between top-level nodes is discarded. For example `<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> <!-- before -->\n<element/>\t<!-- after -->` pretty-prints as four separate lines each with no leading indentation.

Parse errors must be reported as a structured record with three fields on separate lines: `error=parse`, a human-readable `message=` describing what was expected or what mismatch occurred, and `position=` giving the zero-based character offset into the input string where the failure was detected. For empty input the position is 0. For a mismatched close tag like `<foo></bar>`, the position points to the start of the offending close tag.

trigger_keywords: which operations does the document builder accept
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The document builder supports the following operation types: `declaration` (XML declaration with version, optional encoding, and extra attributes), `processing` (processing instruction with target and raw text content), `comment` (comment node with text), `cdata` (CDATA section with text), `text` (literal text node — consecutive text ops within one element are merged), `element` (with name, optional attributes map, optional self-closing preference, and nested children ops array), and `attribute` (name, value, and optional quote style). Childless elements render self-closing unless self-closing is explicitly set to false.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'serialize', 'pretty', 'inspect_element', 'attributes', 'decode', 'encode_text', 'encode_attribute', 'find_elements', 'find_all_elements', 'parse_error', 'build'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the old escaping module handled the double-encoding edge case
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The encode_text function in the entity/character coding module (Feature 5.2) must re-escape ampersands even when they appear inside already-escaped sequences. Input `<foo &>` must produce `<foo &amp;>`, not `<foo &>`. See feature5_2_encode_text.json test case 2.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect in my notes right now. Could you check the test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` for the relevant feature, or look at the existing serializer/parser logic in the codebase for precedent? The behavior should be consistent with how similar edge cases are handled elsewhere in the toolkit.

feature1_round_trip.json.description: Parse a complete XML document from its textual form and serialize it back to a canonical XML string. The serializer normalizes insignificant whitespace inside tags (for example the optional space before a self-closing slash is dropped) while preserving the document's structure, node order, attribute quoting style, character data sections, comments, processing instructions, document type declarations, and namespace prefixes. The output is the canonical textual representation of the parsed tree.

feature2_pretty_print.json.description: Parse an XML document and re-serialize it in a human-readable, indented form. Each nested node is placed on its own line and indented according to its depth; sibling nodes appear on separate lines. The indentation unit defaults to two spaces but can be overridden by supplying an explicit indent string. Insignificant whitespace between nodes is normalized away and replaced by the pretty-printer's own line breaks and indentation.

feature3_element_inspection.json.description: Parse an XML document and report the structural facts of its root element: the element's qualified tag name (including any namespace prefix), the number of attributes it carries, the number of direct child nodes, the total number of descendant nodes (children, their attributes, and deeper descendants), its combined text content (the concatenation of all descendant character data), whether it was written in self-closing form, and its canonical serialized form. An element written as an empty pair of open/close tags is reported as not self-closing, whereas an element written with a single self-closing tag is reported as self-closing.

input.xml: <ns:data key="value">Am I or are the other crazy?</ns:data>

cases.expected_output:
kind=element
name=ns:data
attributes=1
children=1
descendants=2
text=Am I or are the other crazy?
self_closing=true
serialized=<ns:data key="value">Am I or are the other crazy?</ns:data>

kind=element
name=data
attributes=0
children=0
descendants=0
text=
self_closing=false
serialized=<data></data>

feature4_attributes.json.description: Parse an XML element and report, for each of its attributes in document order, the attribute's qualified name, its decoded value (with all character and entity references resolved to their literal characters), the quote style used in the source (single or double), and its canonical serialized form (in which the value is re-escaped as required for the chosen quote style). The count of attributes is reported first. An attribute written with an empty value is reported with an empty value string.

input.xml: <data ns:attr="Am I or are the other crazy?" />

cases.expected_output:
count=1
name=ns:attr
value=Am I or are the other crazy?
quote=double
serialized=ns:attr="Am I or are the other crazy?"

input.xml: <data ns:attr='Am I or are the other crazy?' />

cases.expected_output:
count=1
name=ns:attr
value=Am I or are the other crazy?
quote=single
serialized=ns:attr='Am I or are the other crazy?'

feature5_1_decode.json.description: Resolve character and entity references in a raw string using a selectable entity table. The numeric forms are always recognized: a hexadecimal form (a hash, the letter x in either case, hexadecimal digits) and a decimal form (a hash followed by decimal digits) both resolve to the character at that code point. Named references resolve through the selected table: the minimal table recognizes only the five core XML names; richer tables additionally recognize HTML named references. A reference that is unknown, has no closing delimiter, or is empty is left untouched in the output. The pass-through table performs no resolution at all and returns the input verbatim.

feature5_2_encode_text.json.description: Escape a raw string so that it is safe to embed as XML element text using a selectable entity table. With the standard table the less-than character and the ampersand are replaced by their named references while all other characters, including already-escaped sequences and quotes, are emitted verbatim. The pass-through table performs no escaping and returns the input unchanged.

feature5_3_encode_attribute.json.description: Escape a raw string so that it is safe to embed as an XML attribute value, given the surrounding quote style (single or double). Only the quote character matching the chosen style is escaped to its named reference; the opposite quote character is left verbatim. The ampersand and the less-than character are always escaped, and the tab, newline, and carriage-return characters are escaped to their numeric references. The pass-through table performs no escaping and returns the input unchanged for either quote style.

feature6_1_find_elements.json.description: Find the direct child elements of a context element whose tag matches a requested name and namespace filter, and report the number of matches followed by each matched element's qualified name in document order. The name filter may be a specific local/qualified name or a wildcard that matches any name. When no namespace filter is supplied, the requested name is compared against the element's fully qualified name (prefix included). When a namespace filter is supplied, only the local name is compared and the element's namespace URI must match the filter; a wildcard namespace filter matches elements in any namespace. Only immediate children are considered, never deeper descendants.

feature6_2_find_all_elements.json.description: Find all elements anywhere in the document tree (the root element and every descendant, in document order) whose tag matches a requested name and namespace filter, and report the number of matches followed by each matched element's qualified name. The name and namespace filtering rules are identical to the direct-children search, but here the search is recursive over the whole tree rather than limited to immediate children. A wildcard name combined with no namespace filter therefore enumerates every element in the document.

cases.expected_output:
count=5
name=root
name=group
name=a
name=x:a
name=a

feature7_parse_errors.json.description: Attempt to parse malformed XML and report the failure as a neutral, structured error rather than a successful tree. The report carries a human-readable diagnostic describing what the parser expected at the point of failure and the zero-based character offset into the input where the failure occurred. Conditions covered include: input that does not begin with a tag, trailing content after the root element, a start tag closed by a non-matching end tag, an unterminated tag, and a tag with a missing name.

feature8_build.json.description: Build an XML document programmatically from a sequence of build operations and serialize the result. Supported operations include adding an XML declaration (with version, optional encoding, and extra attributes), a processing instruction (target plus raw content), comments, character-data (CDATA) sections, literal text, elements (with a tag name, an attribute map, an explicit self-closing preference, and nested child operations), and attributes (name, value, and optional quote style). Elements opened with children or text render with explicit open/close tags; childless elements render self-closing unless self-closing is explicitly disabled. Consecutive text additions within one element are merged. The output is the canonical serialization of the assembled document.

cases.expected_output: <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?><?xml-stylesheet href="/style.css" type="text/css" title="default stylesheet"?><bookstore><!--Only one book?--><book><title lang="en">Harry <![CDATA[Potter]]></title><price>29.99</price></book></bookstore>
